main_ingredients: grape juice concentrate or direct-pressed grape juice

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of packaged grape juice is one glass of approximately 250 ml (250 g), providing around 153 kcal and 36 g of sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Grape juice, canned or bottled, unsweetened, without added ascorbic acid — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 167771); cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database entry for Grape juice (packaged)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) taken from HPB Singapore Foodo as primary regional source. USDA SR Legacy used to cross-check and supplement secondary nutrients (potassium, calcium, iron, vitamin C, etc.). Values are consistent between sources.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283. Grape juice entry (GI=53, glucose reference).
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 53 (glucose=100 reference) for unsweetened grape juice is reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (53 × 15.94 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 250 g / 100) / 100 = 21.1 g available carbohydrate per serving × 53 / 100 ≈ 21.1 × 0.53 ≈ 11.2; using standard GL formula: GL = GI/100 × available carbohydrate (g) per serving = 53/100 × 15.94 × 2.5 = 53/100 × 39.85 ≈ 21.1 — reported per serving GL rounded to 21.1 for 250 g; per 100 g GL = 53/100 × 15.94 = 8.4.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (14.5 g/100 g) in a liquid form with no dietary fibre; sugar-sweetened beverage classification independently raises blood pressure risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid packaged grape juice; the 14.5 g/100 g sugar load in liquid form drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidance, despite the low sodium content of 5 mg/100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar (14.5 g/100 g), zero dietary fibre, and liquid form causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation; GI 53 but high glycemic load at typical serving sizes
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid packaged grape juice entirely; despite a moderate GI of 53, the absence of fibre and the liquid sugar matrix cause rapid and significant postprandial glucose elevation, and a 250 ml serving delivers approximately 40 g of available carbohydrate.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free sugar content (14.5 g/100 g) with no fibre; regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with elevated triglycerides and increased CVD risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should limit or avoid packaged grape juice; the high free sugar content (14.5 g/100 g) with zero fibre is associated with elevated serum triglycerides and increased CVD risk when consumed regularly, and whole grapes are a preferable alternative for any polyphenol benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar load (14.5 g/100 g) with zero fibre promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; processing removes skin-bound polyphenols including resveratrol
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid packaged grape juice; the high sugar content promotes inflammatory cytokine activity and the processing removes skin-bound resveratrol, leaving no meaningful anti-inflammatory benefit — whole grapes or small amounts of unsweetened tart cherry juice are preferable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Packaged grape juice is a ready-to-drink beverage produced by pressing grapes, filtering the juice, and pasteurizing it before sealing in cartons, bottles, or pouches. It is widely available in Singapore and globally as a commercial product. The juice has a sweet, slightly tart flavour profile characteristic of Concord or red grape varieties, with a smooth, thin liquid texture and deep purple-red colour. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 61 kcal, 15.94 g carbohydrate, 14.5 g sugar, 0.13 g protein, 0 g fat, 0 g dietary fibre, and 5 mg sodium. The high sugar content (14.5 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre makes it a high-glycaemic-load beverage at typical serving sizes despite a moderate GI of 53. A standard 250 ml serving delivers approximately 40 g of available carbohydrate and over 36 g of sugar. Clinically, packaged grape juice is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage and is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease risk due to rapid postprandial glucose elevation, insulin-mediated sodium retention, and elevated triglyceride risk. Unlike whole grapes, packaged juice lacks dietary fibre and has reduced polyphenol content because processing removes skin-bound resveratrol. It is vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free, with no common allergens. It is best avoided in favour of whole fruit or water for health-conscious consumers.
common_user_queries: Is packaged grape juice high in sugar?
Is grape juice good for blood pressure?
Grape juice vs whole grapes nutrition
Is grape juice a sugar-sweetened beverage?
Can I drink grape juice if I have hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database (primary regional source, confidence 0.95). Secondary nutrients cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 167771) for unsweetened bottled grape juice, which is an exact food match. GI value sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed international GI tables.
notes: Fat_g is confirmed zero per both HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central for unsweetened packaged grape juice — this is a fat-free beverage by formulation (confirmed zero). Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and cholesterol_mg are all confirmed zero for this product category. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero: juice processing removes all grape fibre (confirmed zero per HPB and USDA). Vitamin_b12_mcg and vitamin_d_mcg are confirmed zero as this is a plant-derived beverage with no fortification reported in the reference entry. Calcium_mg value of 11.0 mg/100 g is taken from USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 167771) as the HPB entry reports 0.0 mg; the USDA value is used as the more complete secondary source. Iron_mg value of 0.25 mg/100 g is similarly taken from USDA SR Legacy; HPB reports 0.0 mg. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI/100) × available carbohydrate per serving (g) = (53/100) × (15.94 × 2.5) = 0.53 × 39.85 = 21.1 for a 250 g serving; per-100 g GL = 0.53 × 15.94 = 8.4. The input record listed 'diabetes' as the condition key; this has been updated to 'type2_diabetes' per Veritas naming rules. No ontology codes have been invented; FoodOn URI, FoodEx2 code, and SNOMED CT code are defensible matches for grape juice as a food substance.
